---
title: "I — Electrical foundations"
description: "Safe observation, circuit models, measurement, and dynamic behavior — the habits every later part quietly assumes."
page-role: part
part-id: foundations
---

The first part establishes the habits on which every later result depends:
control the hazard, define what is being measured, choose reference directions,
state the model, and compare its prediction with evidence. Nothing here is
advanced, and that is the point. These eight chapters are the grammar of the
rest of the book; a reader who skips them does not read faster later, only less
carefully.

The part is deliberately bench-first, in the tradition of practical electronics
[@horowitz2015art]. You will meet each idea as an observable behavior before you
meet its equation, predict the behavior, and only then reconcile the prediction
with a measurement. The recurring conviction of the whole book begins here: a
circuit is *one* object described in many languages — the physical assembly and
its energy path, the schematic with named nodes, the governing equations, a
simulation, and the measured waveform with its uncertainty. When those
descriptions agree, you understand the circuit. When they disagree, one of them
is incomplete, invalid under the stated conditions, or reported with too much
confidence. That disagreement is often the most useful thing the bench will tell
you all day.

## The part question

> How can a physical electrical system, a circuit model, and a set of
> measurements be made to tell the same defensible story?

"The same story" means the three agree within a stated uncertainty, using an
explicit reference direction and a model whose validity range you can name.
"Defensible" means you can say which rung of evidence the claim rests on and why
that rung is enough. Part I is the practice of building that agreement for the
simplest systems — a source, a resistor, a capacitor — so that the same
discipline is available when the system is a data logger or a field instrument.

## Why the foundations come first

Every later part inherits four things from this one, silently, and breaks
quietly without them:

- **a safety boundary**, so that energizing a circuit is a staged, reversible
  act rather than a gamble;
- **reference conventions**, so that a voltage polarity and a current direction
  mean something before a sign is written, and a negative result reads as
  "reality opposes my chosen reference," not "error";
- **model limits**, so that every compact formula carries the operating
  conditions that make it true; and
- **an evidence standard**, so that a number is reported with its uncertainty
  rather than to as many digits as the instrument happens to show
  [@jcgm2008gum].

Devices, digital logic, embedded timing, control loops, and product
qualification all assume these habits are already yours. They are capabilities,
not preliminaries to discard.

## Entry knowledge

No prior circuit theory is assumed. Basic algebra and scientific notation are
helpful; reach for [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) the moment
units, graphs, prefixes, or equation manipulation need review, and note that SI
units and their conventions are held to their defining reference throughout the
book [@bipm2019si]. Two further appendices arrive on demand rather than up
front: [M03](../appendices/m03-probability-numerical.qmd) (probability and
statistics) is the companion to measurement and uncertainty in F06, and
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd) (calculus and
differential equations) is the companion to transients in F07. Follow the
prerequisite link when a chapter first needs the technique, then return.

Practical work in this part begins only with current-limited, extra-low-voltage
systems and locally approved procedures. Reading a chapter is never
authorization to energize anything.

## The learning arc

The eight chapters move from *not getting hurt or fooled* to *predicting how a
circuit behaves over time and frequency*. Read them in the order below unless a
prerequisite already held lets you enter later (the dependency graph is recorded
in [`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml)).

| Chapter | Central question it answers | You leave able to | Hands forward |
|---|---|---|---|
| [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) | How do you investigate a system without creating a hazard or corrupting the evidence? | work a bench safely, estimate in SI, and keep a traceable record | the safety boundary and notebook every later chapter assumes |
| [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd) | What is actually moving or changing when a circuit does work? | define charge, voltage, and current with explicit reference directions | the quantities F03–F08 manipulate |
| [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd) | Why does a source's voltage change when a real load is connected? | reason about sources, loads, resistance, and the operating point | Ohm's law, loading, and measurement intrusion |
| [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd) | How can conservation laws predict every node voltage and branch current? | apply Kirchhoff's laws, superposition, and Thévenin/Norton equivalents | the network-reduction habit used everywhere after |
| [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) | Where does energy enter, move through, and leave a system? | build a power budget and reason about ratings, efficiency, and heat | thermal limits that bound every real component |
| [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) | What evidence separates a circuit fault from a measurement mistake? | plan a measurement, state its uncertainty, and isolate a fault by hypothesis | the trustworthy-number standard for the whole book [@jcgm2008gum] |
| [F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd) | How do circuits remember energy and respond when conditions change? | predict first-order RC/RL transients from initial and final values | the dynamics that make timing and filtering possible |
| [F08](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd) | How does one circuit behave differently as signal frequency changes? | use phasors, impedance, transfer functions, and Bode reasoning | frequency-domain thinking for signals, control, and RF [@oppenheim1996signals] |

Grouped by the development they carry, the arc is four movements:

- **F01–F02 — establish safe observation and define the quantities.** Before any
  formula, fix the hazard, the units, and the reference directions. Charge,
  voltage, and current become measurable things with a chosen positive sense,
  not symbols to memorize.
- **F03–F05 — model sources, loads, networks, energy, and heat.** A real source
  sags under load; a network obeys conservation laws; and every component
  dissipates power and has a thermal limit. This movement turns "a circuit" into
  a predictable operating point with a power budget.
- **F06 — decide whether a claim is trustworthy.** The pivot of the part.
  Accuracy, precision, resolution, and uncertainty are separated; a measurement
  is planned rather than improvised; and a fault is isolated by discriminating
  evidence instead of guesswork [@jcgm2008gum].
- **F07–F08 — extend static models to time and frequency.** Stored energy gives
  a circuit memory (transients); sinusoidal steady state gives it a
  frequency-dependent personality (impedance and frequency response). This is
  the bridge from DC intuition to the signals, control, and communication
  chapters later in the book.

## Two spines through the foundations

Page order is one valid path through the dependency graph, not the only one. Two
chains carry most of the part's weight and are worth seeing explicitly:

- **The measurement spine — F01 → F02 → F03 → F06.** It reaches a *trustworthy
  number* as early as possible, because almost every later chapter quietly
  assumes you can produce one. If you must prioritize, prioritize this chain; do
  not skip it to reach a dynamics chapter faster.
- **The dynamics spine — F04 → F07 → F08.** It carries stored energy, transient
  response, impedance, and frequency response, and it flows directly into
  signals and systems in Part V ([S01](../05-domains/s01-signals-systems.qmd))
  [@oppenheim1996signals]. It is the prerequisite for data conversion, feedback
  control, and RF further on.

## How the chapters connect

The genuine dependencies — including the just-in-time appendix hooks — are
smaller than the page order suggests, so a reader who already holds a
prerequisite can enter partway without breaking anything.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-foundation-dependencies
%%| fig-cap: "The direct prerequisite graph for Part I. Each arrow matches one prerequisite in the chapter registry. Appendix nodes are just-in-time inputs; page order, named spines, and downstream handoffs are described separately."
%%| fig-alt: "A vertical prerequisite graph. F01 leads to F02. F02 and M01 lead to F03. F03 leads separately to F04, F05, and F06, while M03 also leads to F06. F04, F05, and M02 lead to F07. F07 and M01 lead to F08."
%%| fig-width: 6.1
flowchart TB
  M01["Appendix · M01<br/>algebra and units"]
  M02["Appendix · M02<br/>calculus and dynamics"]
  M03["Appendix · M03<br/>probability and statistics"]

  F01["F01 · Safe practice"]
  F02["F02 · Electrical quantities"]
  F03["F03 · Operating points"]
  F04["F04 · Networks"]
  F05["F05 · Power and heat"]
  F06["F06 · Measurement"]
  F07["F07 · Transients"]
  F08["F08 · Frequency response"]

  F01 --> F02
  F02 --> F03
  M01 --> F03
  F03 --> F04
  F03 --> F05
  F03 --> F06
  M03 --> F06
  F04 --> F07
  F05 --> F07
  M02 --> F07
  F07 --> F08
  M01 --> F08
```

The arrows in @fig-foundation-dependencies encode direct prerequisites only.
They do not assert that every adjacent page depends on the previous page: the
canonical page order remains F01 through F08, while F04–F06 are parallel
developments after F03. The two spines above are purposeful reader routes. The
measurement spine selects F01, F02, F03, and F06 to reach trustworthy evidence
early; the dynamics spine selects F04, F07, and F08, with F05 and M02 feeding
the transient step and M01 supporting the final frequency-domain step.

## How to read each chapter

Whichever route you take, read every chapter the same way — the working method
the whole book asks of you: **predict, build, measure, reconcile, decide.**
Before a result is revealed, state a directional prediction and its rough
magnitude, and name the model and its limits. Then compare calculation,
simulation where it adds value, and measurement, treating any disagreement as
information rather than noise. Finish by deciding what the evidence actually
permits you to claim — no stronger [@horowitz2015art].

That cycle rides on an evidence ladder that runs from a rough estimate to a
qualified claim:

```text
estimate → calculation → simulation → measurement → repeated test
         → independent reproduction → field evidence → qualified claim
```

Not every question needs the top rung. The consequence of being wrong sets how
much evidence is appropriate: a sign convention can rest on a calculation, while
an energy or safety claim cannot. Choosing the right rung — and stopping there —
is itself part of the engineering, and Part I is where the judgment is first
practiced.

## Turn reading into evidence

Chapters develop durable models; **labs** demand physical evidence, and the
**project** forces several models to succeed together. The aligned practical
sequence for this part is L01–L05, and it integrates into milestone **M1**. All
five foundation labs are current-limited, extra-low-voltage work (safety level
**L1**), but the level, apparatus, stop conditions, and local authorization must
still be defined *before* a lab becomes deliverable.

| Evidence goal | Aligned chapters | Level | What it demonstrates |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L01** Bench safety and instrument qualification | F01, F06 | L1 | you can energize in stages and trust your meters |
| **L02** Source, load, resistance, and meter loading | F02, F03, F06 | L1 | you can measure without silently changing the quantity |
| **L03** Network conservation and equivalents | F04, F06 | L1 | Kirchhoff and Thévenin/Norton predict a real network |
| **L04** Power, efficiency, and temperature rise | F05, F06 | L1 | a power budget matches a measured temperature rise |
| **L05** RC/RL transients and frequency response | F07, F08, S01 | L1 | a time constant and a Bode magnitude agree with the bench |

The integrating milestone is **M1 — measure an energy-and-light system**, which
binds F01–F06 together with reproducible records
([T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd)) into a safe measurement
plan, calibrated observations, and a qualified energy claim. The full practice
map, including how M1 compounds into later milestones, is on the
[practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary"}
Early practical work is deliberately low-voltage and current-limited. Reading a
chapter is not authorization for mains, high-voltage, high-energy battery, RF
power, rotating-machine, medical, or safety-critical work. Use approved
facilities, supervision, and locally approved procedures, and stop when the
evidence or the safety boundary is inadequate.
:::

## Exit capability

After this part you should be able to inspect and energize an approved
low-energy circuit in stages, predict its behavior to the right order of
magnitude, measure without silently changing the quantity of interest, reconcile
a model with its uncertainty, and stop when the evidence or safety boundary is
inadequate. Expressed as a checklist, you can:

- work a bench safely and keep a record another person can reproduce;
- assign reference directions and read a signed result correctly;
- reduce a resistive network and predict its operating point under load;
- build a power budget and respect a component's thermal limit;
- state a measurement with its uncertainty and isolate a fault by hypothesis;
- predict a first-order transient and a first-order frequency response, and know
  the operating range in which each model holds.

## What this hands to the rest of the book

These capabilities feed every later part; they are not scaffolding to be
discarded. Devices and analog build on the operating-point and frequency
reasoning of F03 and F08 to shape and amplify real signals
[@sedra2020microelectronic]; digital hardware treats a logic level as an analog
quantity governed by F05's ratings before it is a bit [@harris2021digital];
feedback and control depend on the transient and frequency response of F07–F08
[@astrom2021feedback]; and product realization inherits F06's evidence standard
as the basis of test, calibration, and electromagnetic compatibility
[@ott2009electromagnetic]. When you can say which model you are using, what would
break it, and how much your evidence lets you claim — for a single voltage — you
can do the same for a system.

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity"}
This is a development edition. The scope and dependency structure of Part I are
complete, and all eight chapters are full teaching drafts. Each chapter reports
its own status in front matter and identifies which examples are calculations,
synthetic teaching data, manufacturer evidence, or work reserved for an aligned
laboratory. A draft remains subject to technical and editorial revision and is
never safety authorization. The [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) explains what
each maturity level lets a reader rely on.
:::
